title: Chinese carrier aviation taking off
gist: IISS analysts compare PLAN carrier-aviation development against the US Navy baseline: China has rapidly built a Fujian-class air wing with the 'five essentials' of a modern carrier force and accelerating night/all-weather training, but its pilot pipeline, recruitment pool and command-track career progression remain far less mature, with surface-warfare officers rather than aviators still dominating carrier command.

insights.claim: Since PLAN commissioned its first carrier, the Liaoning, in September 2012 (two months before Xi Jinping became CCP and Central Military Commission chairman), it has commissioned two more carriers with additional units planned, making carrier development a highly visible, prestige project of Xi-era military modernisation.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Frames carrier aviation as a top-down political priority rather than an incidental capability, implying sustained budget and institutional support through Xi's tenure.
insights.locator: Chinese President Xi Jinping became Chairman

insights.claim: The PLAN has transferred land-based aviation units to the PLA Air Force in recent years specifically to free the navy to concentrate on carrier aviation, signalling a deliberate institutional prioritisation of carrier capability over land-based naval air power.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: A structural reallocation like this is a leading indicator of where PLAN resourcing and career incentives will concentrate next.
insights.locator: Over the past few years land-based aviation units

insights.claim: The carrier Fujian carries the PLAN's largest air wing to date, now including all 'five essentials' of a modern carrier force — low-observable combat aircraft, catapult-assisted multi-role combat aircraft, electronic-warfare aircraft, airborne early warning & control aircraft, and anti-submarine-warfare helicopters — bringing it into close structural parity with a US carrier air wing.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Closing the air-wing composition gap with the US Navy is a concrete capability milestone worth tracking for regional power-projection assessments.
insights.quote: These aircraft types form the ‘five essentials for … carriers’
insights.locator: The carrier Fujian boasts the PLAN’s largest carrier air wing

insights.claim: The PLAN is testing the GJ-21 uninhabited combat aerial vehicle for carrier operations, paralleling the US Navy's MQ-25 Stingray refuelling drone and its Distributed Maritime Operations concept, which could mean the carrier air wing stops being the primary strike package of a carrier strike group in both navies.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: A shift away from the air wing as primary strike package would reshape carrier strike group design and threat calculus for both navies, with implications for uninhabited-systems suppliers.
insights.locator: More recently, the PLAN has begun testing the GJ-21

insights.claim: PLA Daily coverage of PLAN carrier aviation training surged after the Fujian's commissioning: more than 20 pieces on PLAN aviation training were published between October 2025 and mid-2026, versus nine in the equivalent period following the Liaoning's commissioning, indicating a marked acceleration in training tempo and institutional attention.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Media-output volume is a proxy for training intensity; the doubling-plus rate suggests the PLAN is compressing its capability-maturation timeline around the Fujian.
insights.locator: Between October 2025 – a month before the Fujian’s commissioning

insights.claim: PLAN carrier night-operations capability matured over roughly a five-year arc: 2015 reporting described night training as still difficult, 2017 reporting showed the PLAN still struggling with night take-offs and landings, and the PLAN did not conduct its first night-time aerial refuelling mission until 2020.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: A concrete, dated capability timeline useful for benchmarking how quickly the PLAN closes remaining gaps (e.g., all-weather ops) versus the US Navy.
insights.locator: PLA Daily reporting from 2015 highlighted the initial difficulty

insights.claim: The PLAN Carrier Aviation Test and Training Base, established in 2013, likely oversees all carrier pilot training and appears to have driven the improvement in night-time carrier-landing simulation and operations.
insights.speaker: IISS
entities: PLAN Carrier Aviation Test and Training Base
insights.so_what: Identifies the institutional chokepoint for PLAN carrier-pilot quality control, a useful node to watch for leadership changes or expansion.
insights.locator: The PLAN Carrier Aviation Test and Training Base, created in 2013

insights.claim: Cross-academy combat training between the PLA Naval Aviation University and the Dalian Naval Academy was reported by Chinese sources for the first time last year, covering joint elements like vertical replenishment and multi-aircraft collaboration, and is intended to spread carrier aviation expertise beyond a handful of pioneer aviators.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Broadening the trained-pilot base reduces single-point-of-failure risk in PLAN carrier operations and signals scaling intent beyond a small elite cadre.
insights.locator: Beyond night operations, another recent development

themes.theme: Pilot pipeline versus the US model

insights.claim: The PLAN has shifted its carrier-pilot pipeline: initial carrier-pilot training is now conducted by a Naval Aviation University unit for fresh pilots before they earn their wings, whereas the first generation of carrier pilots were experienced aviators specially selected from front-line navy and air-force units.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Moving from hand-picked veterans to a standard training pipeline signals the PLAN believes carrier aviation is mature enough to scale production of pilots rather than rely on scarce elite talent.
insights.locator: Reports suggest that initial carrier-based pilot training

insights.claim: PLAN carrier-pilot age eligibility is markedly tighter than the US Navy's: US Navy pilots may be commissioned between 19-32, while PLAN high-school applicants must be 17-20 and academy graduates must not exceed 24, a constraint the article says has hindered PLAN aviation recruitment numbers.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: A hard demographic/recruitment constraint on PLAN carrier-pilot supply that partly explains reported personnel shortfalls and the push to expand the applicant pool.
insights.locator: Other key differences are the age requirements

insights.claim: The PLA Naval Aviation University's Gaokao entrance threshold is 503/700, placing it in the upper-middle tier of Chinese military academies alongside the Naval Engineering University, and its curriculum is more technical than the broader officer-education focus of the US Naval Academy.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Benchmarks the academic selectivity and orientation of China's carrier-pilot feeder institution against its US counterpart for talent-pipeline comparisons.
insights.locator: Each academy has a different score threshold

insights.claim: Facing demographic headwinds and competition for talent from the civil aviation industry, the PLA is responding by lowering entry requirements and expanding training access for female pilots to widen its carrier-aviation recruitment base.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: A recruitment-constraint response worth monitoring as a leading indicator of how fast the PLAN can staff its planned carrier expansion.

insights.claim: Unlike the US Navy, where naval aviators routinely reach four-star rank and serve as Chief of Naval Operations or combatant commanders, only one PLAN pilot has ever reached Commander of the Navy at Military Region/Theatre Command Leader grade, and no naval pilot has commanded a Military Region or Theatre Command as of writing.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Despite rapid hardware modernisation, the PLAN's aviator community has not yet captured senior command influence the way US naval aviators have — a structural lag worth tracking as a leadership-power indicator distinct from equipment capability.
insights.locator: There is precedent within the US Navy for naval aviators

insights.claim: A current Deputy Commander of the Navy who trained as a pilot is reportedly being considered for the Navy's top job following the removal of the previous commander, though IISS flags this as unconfirmed.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: If confirmed, this would be the first PLAN commander drawn from the aviator community, a signal to watch for a shift in the navy's internal power balance toward carrier aviation.
insights.locator: Some reports suggest that a current Deputy Commander

insights.claim: The 'Pilot-Captain Class' programme retrains carrier pilots as surface-warfare officers to create a new class of carrier commanders, but of nine tracked graduates only one reached Deputy Commander of the Navy and only one was considered (unsuccessfully) for carrier command — known PLAN carrier commanders still come from the surface-warfare branch, in contrast to the US Navy where carriers are typically commanded by naval aviators.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Shows the PLAN's attempt to create an aviator command track has so far largely failed to displace the surface-warfare branch's hold on carrier command — a gap between capability build-out and institutional power that could slow doctrinal innovation.
insights.locator: The careers of these nine graduates vary significantly

insights.claim: The first carrier pilot to land a J-15 went on to become deputy commander then commander of the Carrier Aviation Test and Training Base (2013/2015), Deputy Commander of Theatre Command Naval Aviation (2019), and the first carrier pilot to command a carrier task group as Commander of the Shandong group (2021), before likely becoming Commander of Theatre Command Naval Aviation and being promoted to Vice Admiral in 2026.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: A concrete individual career arc showing the ceiling that PLAN aviators can reach today (Vice Admiral, theatre naval aviation command) short of full navy command — a benchmark for tracking whether the aviator track breaks through further.
insights.locator: Among the most notable naval pilot careers

insights.claim: Per US Department of War reporting cited by IISS, the PLAN plans to commission nine more aircraft carriers by 2035 and is reportedly developing its first nuclear-powered carrier, the Type-004.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: A concrete fleet-expansion trajectory (nine more hulls, first nuclear carrier) that anchors longer-term forecasts of Chinese blue-water power-projection capacity and associated defence-industrial demand.
insights.locator: Based on reporting from the US Department of War

insights.claim: IISS assesses that the central constraint on PLAN carrier aviation going forward is not hardware but limited institutional experience, given how recently the force began developing carrier-aviation capabilities at pace.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Reframes the China-carrier story from a hardware race to an institutional-maturity race, suggesting hardware milestones (new hulls, new aircraft) will outpace the PLAN's ability to fully exploit them for years.
insights.locator: the central issue for the force moving forward may continue to be its limited institutional experience
